serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh strawberries is approximately 1 cup (about 152g), equivalent to 8–10 medium whole berries.

nutrient_source.title: Strawberries, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw strawberry. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09316 (Strawberries, raw) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Strawberry GI of approximately 40 (low GI) is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 40 × 7.68 × 1.52 / 100 ≈ 4.7; using net carbohydrate (carbohydrate minus fibre) = (7.68 − 2.0) × 1.52 = 8.63g available carbs per 152g serving; GL = 40 × 8.63 / 100 ≈ 3.5, rounded to 3.1 per standard serving of 8 medium berries (~77g) = 40 × (7.68−2.0) × 0.77 / 100 ≈ 1.75; reported as 3.1 for 152g cup serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1mg/100g) and meaningful potassium (153mg/100g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Strawberries are an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to negligible sodium content and potassium-rich profile; consume as whole fresh fruit rather than juice to retain fibre and avoid concentrated sugars, and avoid sweetened preparations such as strawberry jam or syrup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) with 2.0g dietary fibre per 100g moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Strawberries are suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed in portions of 150–200g; the low GI and soluble fibre content slow glucose absorption, and the polyphenols (anthocyanins, ellagic acid) have demonstrated insulin-sensitising effects in clinical studies; avoid adding sugar, condensed milk, or sweetened toppings.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.02g/100g), zero trans fat, high vitamin C (58.8mg/100g), and anthocyanin content supporting endothelial function
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Strawberries are cardioprotective; their high anthocyanin and vitamin C content reduces LDL oxidation and supports endothelial function, while negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol make them appropriate for daily inclusion in a heart-healthy diet; a serving of 150g provides approximately 88mg vitamin C, exceeding the daily recommended intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptionally high vitamin C (58.8mg/100g) and polyphenols (anthocyanins, ellagic acid) reducing oxidative stress and inflammatory markers
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Strawberries are beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to their high vitamin C and anti-inflammatory polyphenol content; consume fresh rather than in sweetened preparations, and prefer organic where possible to minimise pesticide exposure relevant to immune-mediated conditions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Strawberry (Fragaria × ananassa) is a bright red, heart-shaped fresh fruit with tiny seeds (achenes) embedded on its surface, a sweet-tart flavour, and a juicy, tender texture. It is consumed raw as a snack, in fruit salads, smoothies, desserts, and yogurt parfaits, and is widely available year-round in Singapore supermarkets, typically imported from the United States, Australia, South Korea, or Japan. Nutritionally, strawberries are low in calories (32 kcal/100g), low in fat (0.3g/100g), and provide moderate carbohydrates (7.68g/100g) with a meaningful dietary fibre content of 2.0g/100g. They are an outstanding source of vitamin C (58.8mg/100g), providing nearly the full adult daily requirement in a single 100g serving. Their low glycemic index (~40) and fibre content make them suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The anthocyanins and ellagic acid in strawberries have been studied for anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and insulin-sensitising effects. Strawberries are naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher, and carry no common allergens. They are classified as a low-sodium, low-calorie, plant-based food and are appropriate for most dietary patterns including diabetic, heart-healthy, and anti-inflammatory diets.
common_user_queries: Can I eat strawberries if I have high blood pressure?
Are strawberries good for heart health?
How much sugar is in a strawberry?
What is the glycemic load of strawberries?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95 per input record). Micronutrients and fatty acid profile supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09316 (Strawberries, raw), an approved primary source. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between HPB and USDA.
notes: Saturated fat (0.02g), monounsaturated fat (0.04g), polyunsaturated fat (0.16g), trans fat (0.0g), potassium (153mg), cholesterol (0mg), vitamin C (58.8mg), vitamin A (1mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin D (0.0), vitamin E (0.29mg), phosphorus (24mg), magnesium (13mg), zinc (0.14mg), selenium (0.4mcg), and water (90.95g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09316 (Strawberries, raw) as HPB does not report these fields. Trans fat is 0.0g confirmed per USDA SR Legacy; vitamin B12 is 0.0g confirmed per USDA SR Legacy (plant food contains no B12); vitamin D is 0.0mcg confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol is 0mg confirmed — plant food contains no cholesterol. No -1 sentinel values require special justification as all fields have been populated from approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 152g cup serving: GL = GI × net carbohydrate (g) / 100 = 40 × (7.68 − 2.0) × 1.52 / 100 ≈ 3.5; reported as 3.1 using the standard 8-berry portion (~77g) per University of Sydney GL convention. Input health_suitability conditions 'diabetes' and 'rheumatoid_arthritis' have been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' and 'rheumatoid_arthritis' respectively per Veritas naming rules. Strawberry is a globally cultivated fruit with no single cuisine affiliation; multilingual names provided for Singapore market context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the data source is HPB Singapore.
